page.title: nephrolithiasis — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for nephrolithiasis: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for nephrolithiasis: 127 alterations and 268 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

gmi_articles.title: A higher dietary magnesium intake is associated with a reduced risk of kidney stone disease.

Among healthy postmenopausal women, although calcium with vitamin D supplementation resulted in a small but significant improvement in hip bone density, it did not significantly reduce hip fracture, and increased the risk of kidney stones.
Blackcurrant juice could support the metaphylaxis and treatment of uric acid urolithiasis because of its alkalizing effect.
Calcium stone-forming patients have lower mean levels of vitamin D and a higher percentage of hypovitaminosis D than in non-stone-forming patients.
Hypomagnesemia and hypocitraturia associated with PPI exposure may contribute to kidney stone risk.
Lactobacillus casei and Bifidobacterium breve reduce urinary oxalate excretion in nephrolithiasis patients.
Lemonade therapy appears to be a reasonable alternative for patients with hypocitraturic nephrolithiasis.
Melamine-tainted milk-based infant formula consumption is linked to nephrolithiasis.
Mineral water rich in calcium, magnesium and bicarbonate improves urine composition and reduces the risk of calcium oxalate formation.
Mineral waters containing bicarbonate reduce the risk of urinary stone formation in patients with multiple episodes of calcium oxalate urinary stones.
N. sativa is demonstrated to have significant positive effects on disappearance or reduction of size of kidney stones.
Omega-3 fatty acids prevent the formation of urinary calcium oxalate stone formation.
Orange juice may have a therapeutic effect in reducing stone-forming risk.
Potassium citrate reduces kidney-stone incidence in children treated with a ketogenic diet for intractable epilepsy.
Taking calcium supplements without food/at bedtime may increase the risk of calcium oxalate nephrolithiasis.
The Ayurvedic herb Kulattha is superior to potassium citrate in treating kidney calcification disease.
The consumption of bottled water is associated with heightened risk for certain health conditions.
The leaf juice of S. grandiflora demonstrates significant antiurolithiatic activity against calcium oxalate-type stones.
Vitamin E has an ameliorative effect on oxidative stress associated with kidney stone formation in tuberculosis patients.
Selenized astragalus polysaccharide may be a candidate drug for the treatment and prevention of kidney stones.
"A child with atypical celiac disease and recurrent urolithiasis."
Hyperoside ameliorates renal tubular oxidative damage and calcium oxalate deposition.
Melatonin exerts a protective effect in ameliorating nephrolithiasis.
Phaseolus vulgaris L. green bean pod extract demonstrated significant anti-urolithiatic activity.
PPARG: A novel target for yellow tea in kidney stone prevention.
A water extract of Hibiscus sabdariffa significantly lowers the deposition of stone-forming constituents in the kidneys and serum of urolithiatic rats.
Allicin attenuates calcium oxalate crystal deposition.
Brown seaweed extract demonstrates significant prevention effects on kidney and urinary bladder stone formation in rats.
Carvacrol reduced pathological and biochemical damage, and improved kidney function by lowering the serum urea level.
Curcumin prevents shock-wave lithotripsy-induced renal injury.
Effect of carnosine on the course of experimental urate nephrolithiasis.
Evening primrose may have preventive and therapeutic value in infection-induced kidney stones.
Fructose may be a contributing factor for the development of kidney stones in subjects with metabolic syndrome and those suffering from heat stress.
Hibiscus sabdariffa and Phyllanthus amaraus inhibit calcium oxalate stone formation in the rats.
Luban has shown a significant improvement in the treatment and prevention of experimentally induced renal stones.
Moringa oleifera has a preventive and curative effect on calcium oxalate stone formation (urolithiasis) in rats.
O. grandiflorum has a significant inhibitory effect on crystal deposition in the calcium oxalate-stone-forming model.
Phyllanthus niruri inhibits crystal growth in renal stone formation.
Plantain and crataeva nurvala (Varuna) has therapeutic value in the management of upper urinary-tract calculi, especially renal calculi.
Pomegranate juice has a protective effect on kidney stone formation (nephrolithiasis) in rats.

disclaimer: Alterations and therapeutics are aggregated from public databases (Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, DGIdb, and others). Natural agents are candidates from OSMF narrative reviews on opensourcemed.info/chronic-disease.html — not approved treatments. This page is for research orientation only — not medical advice.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous passage without intervention: 70-80% of stones <4 mm; 40-50% of stones 4-6mm; <25% of stones >6mm; recurrence in 50% within 10 years without metabolic assessment and prevention
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin, 0.4mg): facilitate passage 30-45% faster for distal ureteral stones; ESWL: 75-80% stone-free for <2cm renal stones; PCNL: 90%+ stone-free for >2cm stones; dietary and metabolic prevention: 50% recurrence reduction with hydration + diet (STOP-JNKi-like approach)
remission.gap_size: Moderate — metabolic evaluation and dietary prevention are highly effective but chronically underperformed after first stone
remission.barrier_detail: Only 10-20% of first-time kidney stone patients receive a 24-hour urine metabolic evaluation that would guide specific dietary and pharmacological prevention; high fluid intake (>2.5L/day) is evidence-based and free but rarely systematically emphasised; recurrence is highly preventable with appropriate dietary modification
remission.last_soc_change: 2019 — AUA/EAU guidelines updated on 24-hour urine evaluation (recommend for all recurrent stone formers, single stone in high-risk); ESWL vs URS evidence updated; no pharmacological paradigm change; thiazide diuretics and citrate supplementation standard since 1980s
remission.outdated_treatments: ESWL for all stone types and locations (cystine, brushite, large stones — poor ESWL results; URS or PCNL preferred); routine urine culture without 24-hour metabolic evaluation in high-risk patients; opioid analgesia as first-line (NSAIDs proven equivalent and reduce stone passage time via ureteral muscle relaxation)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: High fluid intake (≥2.5L/day — reduces recurrence 50% as primary intervention); dietary counselling (low sodium, moderate calcium, low animal protein — prevents all major stone types); lemon juice (increases urinary citrate in hypocitraturia — comparable to potassium citrate in small trials); Finacea (azelaic acid) for uric acid stones
remission.frontier_signal: Imaging-guided percutaneous lithotripsy with single-access approach expanding; lithotripsy gel phase change technology (prevents haematoma); robotic-assisted URS; CRISPR targeting SLC3A1 for cystinuria; vapour-based ablation for staghorn calculi
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
